entries.title: The Shepherd's present care
entries.body: JFB highlights the Psalmist's confidence in the Lord's constant provision. The green pastures and still waters picture not mere rest, but the Shepherd's wise care that restores, guides, and keeps His people in the right path for His name's sake.

entries.title: Christ's rest is received by coming to Him
entries.body: JFB emphasizes that the rest Christ offers is not passive ease, but relief for burdened souls who come under His gracious rule. His yoke is easy because He Himself carries the weight and teaches His people gently.

entries.title: Boldness grows from Christ's priestly sympathy
entries.body: JFB points out that confidence before the throne of grace rests on Christ's priesthood and His real sympathy with human weakness. The passage invites weary believers not to withdraw, but to come boldly for mercy and timely help.

entries.title: Hearing must become doing
entries.body: JFB stresses that the danger is not simply forgetting information but deceiving oneself by stopping at hearing. The Word is meant to be looked into with perseverance until it shapes obedient action, and the blessing promised is tied to doing what God says.

entries.title: God asks for humble obedience, not display
entries.body: JFB reads Micah's answer as a summary of true religion: justice toward others, mercy from the heart, and a humble walk with God. The verse strips away religious show and brings the soul back to simple, covenant faithfulness.

entries.title: The Word must clothe the whole life
entries.body: JFB notes that Paul moves from doctrine to the garments of a renewed life: mercy, forgiveness, love, peace, and thanksgiving. The dwelling of Christ's word within the church is meant to overflow in worship and in ordinary obedience.

entries.title: The risen Christ draws near before He explains
entries.body: JFB notes that Jesus joins the disciples while they are confused and sad, drawing out their grief before opening the Scriptures. The scene shows Christ's gentle way of meeting discouraged hearts and leading them from perplexity into understanding.

entries.title: Christ interprets all Scripture through Himself
entries.body: JFB treats this moment as a rebuke full of mercy: Christ corrects slow hearts by tracing the necessity of His sufferings and glory through the Scriptures. The point is not bare information, but a renewed reading of the whole Bible in the light of Christ.

entries.title: The gift of the Son reveals the heart of God
entries.body: JFB reads these verses as the fullest declaration of God's love in the giving of His Son. The point is not merely that salvation is offered, but that condemnation is not Christ's mission here; He is sent so that those who believe might live.

entries.title: Fruitfulness depends on abiding union
entries.body: JFB emphasizes that fruit does not come from self-effort alone, but from living union with Christ the true vine. The repeated call to abide means dependence, perseverance, and the abandonment of self-sufficiency.

entries.title: Pressing on means counting Christ as greater
entries.body: JFB highlights Paul's holy dissatisfaction: he has gained Christ, yet still presses on to know Him more fully. The passage calls believers away from self-confidence and toward a forward-leaning pursuit of Christ.

entries.title: Endurance comes from looking to Jesus
entries.body: JFB points to the race imagery as a summons to cast off hindrances and endure by fixing the eye on Christ. The believer's strength is not found in self-generated resolve, but in considering the endurance of Jesus Himself.

entries.title: Waiting on the Lord is active hope
entries.body: JFB reads the Psalmist's waiting as courage rooted in God's goodness, not resignation. Faith steadies the heart by expecting the Lord to act even when present circumstances feel thin.

entries.title: Renewed strength comes by waiting, not striving
entries.body: JFB treats this promise as a rebuke to self-reliance. The weary are renewed not by frantic effort, but by depending on the everlasting God whose strength does not fail.

entries.title: Hope rises from the Lord's unspent mercies
entries.body: JFB notes that Jeremiah anchors hope in God's covenant compassion rather than in changing feelings. Fresh mercy each morning teaches the soul to begin again with the Lord as its portion.

entries.title: Steady rootedness grows beside the Word
entries.body: JFB contrasts a drifting life with one planted by God's instruction. The blessed man endures because meditation keeps his roots in the stream of divine truth.

entries.title: Perseverance keeps doing good in season and out
entries.body: JFB emphasizes that weariness is one of the believer's ordinary temptations. Paul counters it with the promise that patient, active love toward others is never wasted before God.

entries.title: Obedience proves the foundation
entries.body: JFB presses the force of Jesus' question: profession without obedience cannot withstand testing. The wise builder is the one who hears Christ and acts on His words down where no one else can see.

entries.title: Love shows itself in practiced devotion
entries.body: JFB highlights how genuine love becomes visible in zeal, patience, prayer, generosity, and hospitality. Christian affection is not sentimental language but a life arranged around serving Christ and His people.

entries.title: Grace reshapes speech and relationships
entries.body: JFB notes that the new life reaches the tongue and the temper alike. Believers are called to put away corrosive speech and embody the forgiving kindness they have received in Christ.

entries.title: Christlike humility stoops toward others
entries.body: JFB treats this command as a call to renounce self-importance by looking to the interests of others. The mind of Christ becomes the pattern for practical love and mutual care.

entries.title: Love must move from speech into deed
entries.body: JFB points out that Christ's self-giving love becomes the measure of ours. Real love does not stop with tender words; it opens the hand and acts in truth for a brother in need.

entries.title: Understanding comes through clear exposition
entries.body: JFB sees Ezra and the Levites modeling faithful ministry: the Word is read distinctly, explained carefully, and applied to the people's hearts. Understanding is meant to end in holy joy, not mere information.

entries.title: The storm reveals who Christ is
entries.body: JFB notes that the miracle exposes both the disciples' fear and Jesus' divine authority. The question is not only why the storm ceased, but what kind of Lord commands wind and sea.

entries.title: Understanding is learned in the path of obedience
entries.body: JFB points out that the Psalmist asks not for abstract knowledge but for a heart inclined to walk in God's way. Right understanding includes desire, direction, and the turning of the eyes from vanity.

entries.title: Scripture is opened by being led to Jesus
entries.body: JFB highlights the eunuch's humility and Philip's clarity. The passage shows that faithful explanation does not stop with the text itself, but brings the reader to Christ as its center.

entries.title: Scripture makes the believer wise and ready
entries.body: JFB emphasizes that Scripture is God-breathed for more than information. It forms, corrects, and equips the servant of God for faithful living.

entries.title: Growth rests in Christ's unbreakable love
entries.body: JFB sees these verses as a summit of assurance: the believer grows not by anxiety but by resting in the love of God secured in Christ. No opposing force can sever what grace has joined.

entries.title: Grace supplies what growth requires
entries.body: JFB notes that divine power provides both the promises and the resources for godliness. The call to add virtue after faith is not self-salvation, but diligent participation in grace.

entries.title: Continue in Christ the way you began
entries.body: JFB emphasizes that Christian maturity is not a move beyond Christ but a deeper rooting in Him. Thanksgiving marks the soul that is being built up by the same grace that first received Him.

entries.title: Strengthened hearts receive Christ's vast love
entries.body: JFB sees Paul praying for inward strengthening so believers can begin to comprehend the immeasurable love of Christ. Growth here is spiritual enlargement, not mere activity.

entries.title: Growth shows up as the Spirit's fruit
entries.body: JFB notes that the Spirit forms a recognizable pattern of life in those who belong to Christ. Walking in the Spirit means letting that inner life rule the outward step.
